examples.title: Basic Set Operations - Union, Intersection, Difference
examples.description: Demonstrates fundamental set operations using Python's built-in set data type
examples.code:
# Define two sets
A = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}
B = {4, 5, 6, 7, 8}

# Union (A ∪ B) - All elements from both sets
union_set = A | B
print(f"Union A ∪ B: {union_set}")

# Intersection (A ∩ B) - Common elements
intersection_set = A & B
print(f"Intersection A ∩ B: {intersection_set}")

# Difference (A - B) - Elements in A but not in B
difference_set = A - B
print(f"Difference A - B: {difference_set}")

# Symmetric Difference - Elements in either set but not both
symmetric_diff = A ^ B
print(f"Symmetric Difference: {symmetric_diff}")

# Set equality
print(f"A == B: {A == B}")
examples.explanation: Python's set data type directly supports mathematical set operations: | (union), & (intersection), - (difference), and ^ (symmetric difference). These operations correspond exactly to the set theory operations studied in discrete mathematics. The code demonstrates each operation with concrete examples using sets A and B.
examples.timeComplexity: O(min(len(A), len(B))) for intersection; O(len(A) + len(B)) for union, difference, symmetric difference

examples.title: Subset, Superset, and Power Set Operations
examples.description: Demonstrates set relationships and the power set operation
examples.code:
from itertools import combinations

# Define sets for subset/superset demonstrations
X = {1, 2, 3}
Y = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}
empty_set = set()

# Check subset relationship (X ⊆ Y)
print(f"X ⊆ Y: {X.issubset(Y)}")  # True - X is subset of Y
print(f"Y ⊆ X: {Y.issubset(X)}")  # False
print(f"X ⊂ X: {X < X}")         # False - proper subset check

# Check superset relationship (Y ⊇ X)
print(f"Y ⊇ X: {Y.issuperset(X)}")
print(f"X ⊇ Y: {X.issuperset(Y)}")

# Empty set is subset of all sets
print(f"∅ ⊆ X: {empty_set.issubset(X)}")

# Power Set - All subsets of a set
def power_set(s):
    """Generate power set using combination formula |P(A)| = 2^n"""
    power = [set()]
    for elem in s:
        new_subsets = [subset | {elem} for subset in power]
        power.extend(new_subsets)
    return power

original_set = {1, 2, 3}
ps = power_set(original_set)
print(f"\nPower Set of {{1,2,3}}:")
for subset in ps:
    print(f"  {subset}")
print(f"|P(A)| = 2^{len(original_set)} = {len(ps)}")
examples.explanation: This example covers important set relationship operations: issubset(), issuperset() for checking subset/superset relationships, and proper subset (<) checks. The power set function demonstrates the fundamental theorem that |P(A)| = 2^n where n is the cardinality of the set. The algorithm generates all 2^n subsets through iterative combination building.
examples.timeComplexity: O(2^n) for power set generation; O(1) for subset/superset checks

examples.title: Cartesian Product and Set Builder Notation
examples.description: Demonstrates Cartesian products and set comprehension for complex set operations
examples.explanation: This advanced example demonstrates Cartesian products using set comprehensions (matching set-builder notation), the fundamental property |A × B| = |A| × |B|, and verification of De Morgan's Laws using set operations. The set comprehension syntax {expression for variable in set if condition} directly implements mathematical set-builder notation. The example shows how Python can verify theoretical set theory identities.
examples.timeComplexity: O(|A| × |B|) for Cartesian product; O(n) for set comprehensions with filtering
